title: HFpEF Diagnosis: Echo Criteria & Scoring (HFA-PEFF / H₂FPEF)
subtitle: Bedside echocardiographic major/minor criteria, the HFA-PEFF and H₂FPEF probability scores, biomarker caveats, and resolving the intermediate zone with stress echo and invasive haemodynamics
authors: User-provided clinical summary (thresholds verified against HFA-PEFF consensus, Pieske et al. Eur Heart J 2019, and the H2FPEF score, Reddy et al. Circulation 2018)

items.title: How HFpEF is diagnosed — the three mandatory criteria
items.content:
A definitive HFpEF diagnosis is not made on symptoms alone — it requires all three of: (1) symptoms and/or signs of heart failure (exertional dyspnoea, orthopnoea, PND, fatigue, peripheral oedema); (2) a preserved ejection fraction, LVEF ≥ 50% on a stable, good-quality echo, with no major uncorrected valvular disease; and (3) objective evidence of cardiac dysfunction — non-invasive or invasive documentation of elevated LV filling pressures, or structural/functional alterations of diastolic dysfunction.
The diagnostic difficulty is that the dominant symptom (exertional dyspnoea) is highly non-specific and overlaps with obesity, lung disease and deconditioning, and resting echocardiography is often unrevealing — so a structured, multi-parametric approach is needed.
keyPoints: All three required: HF symptoms/signs + LVEF ≥ 50% + objective cardiac dysfunction
Exclude major uncorrected valvular pathology before labelling HFpEF
Dyspnoea is non-specific; resting echo is frequently normal — use structured scoring
items.reference: Defining the Diagnostic Framework: "a definitive diagnosis of HFpEF requires meeting all three of the following criteria: 1. Symptoms and/or Signs of Heart Failure... 2. Preserved Ejection Fraction: LVEF ≥ 50%... 3. Objective Evidence of Cardiac Dysfunction."

items.title: How the HFA-PEFF echo criteria split into Functional and Structural domains, with Major (2 pts) and Minor (1 pt) criteria
items.content:
The HFA-PEFF echocardiographic evaluation is organised across two core domains: Functional (active diastolic pressures) and Structural (chronic remodelling). Within each domain, any single Major criterion scores 2 points and any single Minor criterion scores 1 point.
Functional Major criteria: average E/e′ ≥ 15; septal e′ < 7 cm/s OR lateral e′ < 10 cm/s (in patients < 75 years); peak TR velocity > 2.8 m/s. Functional Minor criteria: average E/e′ 9–14; LV global longitudinal strain (GLS) < 16%.
Structural Major criteria: LAVI > 34 mL/m² (sinus rhythm); LVMI ≥ 115 g/m² (men) or ≥ 95 g/m² (women); relative wall thickness (RWT) > 0.42 with elevated mass (concentric hypertrophy). Structural Minor criteria: LAVI 29–34 mL/m² (sinus rhythm); LAVI > 40 mL/m² in atrial fibrillation; RWT > 0.42 with normal LVMI (concentric remodelling).
keyPoints: Two domains — Functional and Structural; Major = 2 pts, Minor = 1 pt
Functional Major: E/e′ ≥ 15, septal e′ < 7 / lateral e′ < 10, TR velocity > 2.8 m/s
Structural Major: LAVI > 34 (SR), LVMI ≥ 115 (M)/≥ 95 (W), RWT > 0.42 + high mass
items.reference: Functional Domain (Diastolic Alterations & Pressures): "Major Criteria (2 Points Each)... Average E/e' ratio ≥ 15... Septal e' velocity < 7 cm/s OR Lateral e' velocity < 10 cm/s... Peak Tricuspid Regurgitation (TR) velocity > 2.8 m/s."

items.title: Why E/e′, e′ velocity and TR velocity are the major functional surrogates for filling pressure
items.content:
The functional criteria are non-invasive surrogates for elevated LV end-diastolic pressure (LVEDP) and pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP).
The average E/e′ ratio (averaging septal and lateral early diastolic mitral annular velocities) rises as filling pressure rises — ≥ 15 strongly indicates elevated filling pressures, while 9–14 is intermediate.
e′ velocity falls with impaired myocardial relaxation, so a low septal (< 7) or lateral (< 10) e′ flags diastolic dysfunction.
Peak TR velocity > 2.8 m/s translates to an estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure > 31–36 mmHg (assuming a normal right atrial pressure of 5 mmHg) — a downstream marker of chronically elevated left-sided pressures, provided primary lung disease is excluded.
keyPoints: E/e′ and e′ are tissue-Doppler surrogates for LVEDP/PCWP
TR velocity > 2.8 m/s ≈ PASP > 31–36 mmHg (RAP 5 mmHg) — exclude primary lung disease
E/e′ ≥ 15 = strong; 9–14 = intermediate
items.reference: Functional Domain (Diastolic Alterations & Pressures): "These parameters serve as non-invasive surrogates for elevated Left Ventricular End-Diastolic Pressure (LVEDP) and Pulmonary Capillary Wedge Pressure (PCWP)."

items.title: GLS < 16% — subtle systolic dysfunction hiding behind a normal ejection fraction
items.content:
LV global longitudinal strain (GLS) < 16% is a Minor functional criterion (1 point). It demonstrates subtle longitudinal systolic dysfunction despite a preserved global volumetric ejection fraction — i.e., the long-axis myocardial fibres are already failing even though the ejection fraction looks normal.
The other Minor functional criterion is an average E/e′ in the intermediate 9–14 range.
keyPoints: GLS < 16% = Minor functional criterion (1 pt)
Reveals longitudinal systolic impairment despite preserved LVEF
Average E/e′ 9–14 is the other Minor functional criterion
items.reference: Minor Criteria (1 Point Each): "Left Ventricular Global Longitudinal Strain (GLS) < 16% (Demonstrates subtle longitudinal systolic dysfunction despite a preserved global volumetric ejection fraction)."

items.title: Why left atrial size and LV mass capture the chronic footprint of high filling pressures
items.content:
Chronic exposure to elevated filling pressures alters myocardial geometry and stretches the left atrium, so the structural criteria record the cumulative remodelling that diastolic dysfunction leaves behind.
Left atrial volume index (LAVI) > 34 mL/m² in sinus rhythm is a Major criterion — the left atrium acts as a chronic 'barometer' of LV filling pressure.
LV mass index (LVMI) ≥ 115 g/m² (men) or ≥ 95 g/m² (women) reflects hypertrophy from chronic pressure load.
Relative wall thickness (RWT) > 0.42 distinguishes the geometry: with elevated mass it confirms concentric hypertrophy (Major), while with normal mass it indicates concentric remodelling (Minor).
keyPoints: LAVI > 34 mL/m² (SR) = Major — the LA is a chronic barometer of filling pressure
LVMI ≥ 115 (M)/≥ 95 (W) g/m² = Major
RWT > 0.42 + high mass = concentric hypertrophy (Major); + normal mass = concentric remodelling (Minor)
items.reference: Structural Domain (Remodeling Alterations): "Chronic exposure to elevated filling pressures alters myocardial geometry and causes left atrial stretching."

items.title: Why diagnostic thresholds shift upward in atrial fibrillation
items.content:
Chronic atrial fibrillation inherently dilates the left atrium and raises natriuretic peptides regardless of ventricular pathology, so several thresholds are shifted upward to avoid over-diagnosis.
LAVI: in AF the structural threshold moves to > 40 mL/m² (vs > 34 mL/m² in sinus rhythm), and a LAVI > 40 mL/m² in AF is treated as a Minor structural criterion.
Natriuretic peptides: the NT-proBNP cut-point used to trigger work-up rises in AF (the source uses > 365 pg/mL in AF vs > 125 pg/mL in sinus rhythm). (See the Skepticism note for how these map onto the HFA-PEFF biomarker domain's Major thresholds of ≥ 220 / ≥ 660 pg/mL.)
keyPoints: AF dilates the LA and raises NPs independent of LV disease — thresholds move up
LAVI threshold: > 40 mL/m² in AF (Minor) vs > 34 mL/m² in sinus rhythm (Major)
NT-proBNP referral cut-point rises in AF (> 365 vs > 125 pg/mL)
items.reference: Minor Criteria (1 Point Each): "LAVI > 40 mL/m² if the patient is in Atrial Fibrillation (Threshold shifted upward because chronic AF inherently dilates the atrium regardless of ventricular pathology)."

items.title: How the HFA-PEFF score is interpreted across its three domains
items.content:
The HFA-PEFF score (ESC/HFA consensus, Pieske et al. 2019) evaluates three domains — Functional (echo Doppler), Morphological/Structural (LAVI, LV mass), and Biomarkers (natriuretic peptides) — scoring each domain up to 2 points (max 6).
Interpretation: a total ≥ 5 rules in (confirms) HFpEF; 0–1 makes HFpEF unlikely (rule out); and an intermediate score of 2–4 is indeterminate and mandates further functional testing (diastolic stress echo or invasive haemodynamics).
A practical strength is that the score can still be calculated when not every parameter is available.
keyPoints: Three domains (Functional, Morphological, Biomarker), each up to 2 points (max 6)
≥ 5 rules in; 0–1 rules out; 2–4 is intermediate → escalate to functional testing
Calculable even with incomplete parameters
items.reference: The HFA-PEFF Score (ESC/HFA Consensus Algorithm): "It scores each domain up to 2 points. A score of ≥ 5 confirms HFpEF, while a score of 2–4 falls into the intermediate zone."

items.title: How the H₂FPEF score estimates probability from bedside clinical variables
items.content:
The H₂FPEF score (Reddy et al., Circulation 2018) is a heavily weighted clinical/echo rule: H — Heavy, BMI > 30 kg/m² (2 points); H — Hypertensive, on ≥ 2 antihypertensive medications (1 point); F — atrial Fibrillation, paroxysmal or persistent (3 points); P — Pulmonary hypertension, Doppler PASP > 35 mmHg (1 point); E — Elder, age > 60 years (1 point); F — Filling pressures, echocardiographic E/e′ > 9 (1 point). The total ranges 0–9.
Interpretation: a score ≥ 6 confirms HFpEF and ≤ 1 effectively rules it out, with 2–5 intermediate. Atrial fibrillation is the single strongest predictor (3 points).
NOTE: the verified published PASP cut-point for the 'P' variable is > 35 mmHg — the pasted source stated > 40 mmHg, which is incorrect (see the Skepticism note).
keyPoints: H₂FPEF: Heavy (BMI>30, 2) · Hypertensive (≥2 meds, 1) · Fibrillation (3) · Pulmonary HTN (PASP>35, 1) · Elder (>60, 1) · Filling (E/e′>9, 1)
≥ 6 rules in; ≤ 1 rules out; AF is the most heavily weighted single variable
Use PASP > 35 mmHg (verified), not the source's > 40 mmHg
items.reference: The H2FPEF Score (Clinical/Echo Rule): "A score of ≥ 6 confirms the diagnosis; ≤ 1 effectively rules it out... F Atrial Fibrillation: Paroxysmal or Persistent 3 [points]."

items.title: Why obesity and atrial fibrillation both distort natriuretic peptide interpretation
items.content:
Natriuretic peptides (NT-proBNP/BNP) are central to HFpEF work-up but have two important traps.
Obesity: obese patients frequently have falsely suppressed natriuretic peptide levels due to increased clearance — so a 'normal' NT-proBNP does NOT exclude HFpEF in a patient with a high BMI. This matters because obesity is one of the commonest HFpEF phenotypes.
Atrial fibrillation: AF independently raises natriuretic peptides, so the threshold must be adjusted upward (the source uses NT-proBNP > 365 pg/mL in AF vs > 125 pg/mL in sinus rhythm) to avoid false positives.
The practical rule: interpret a single NP value against the patient's BMI and rhythm, never in isolation.
keyPoints: Obesity falsely lowers NPs (increased clearance) — a normal NT-proBNP does not exclude HFpEF
AF raises NPs — use a higher threshold in AF
Always interpret NT-proBNP in the context of BMI and rhythm
items.reference: Standard Resting Biomarkers & Diagnostics: "obese patients frequently exhibit falsely suppressed natriuretic peptide levels due to increased clearance, meaning a 'normal' NT-proBNP does not exclude HFpEF in the presence of a high body mass index."

items.title: How to resolve an indeterminate score — diastolic stress echo, then invasive haemodynamics
items.content:
When resting indicators or scores are intermediate (e.g. only Minor echo criteria, HFA-PEFF 2–4, borderline NT-proBNP), dynamic testing is mandatory because diastolic abnormalities may appear only under physiological stress.
Diastolic stress echocardiography (exercise echo) tracks E/e′ and TR velocity during supine ergometry or treadmill: a stress-induced average E/e′ ≥ 15, or a peak TR velocity > 3.4 m/s, fulfils the criteria for a definitive diagnosis.
Invasive haemodynamic exercise testing is the gold standard when non-invasive testing is inconclusive but suspicion is high: a resting PCWP ≥ 15 mmHg, or an exercise-induced peak PCWP ≥ 25 mmHg, gives absolute confirmation of HFpEF.
keyPoints: Intermediate score/criteria → escalate; diastolic dysfunction may only show on stress
Stress echo positive: average E/e′ ≥ 15 or peak TR velocity > 3.4 m/s
Invasive gold standard: resting PCWP ≥ 15 mmHg or exercise peak PCWP ≥ 25 mmHg
items.reference: Invasive Hemodynamic Exercise Testing (The Gold Standard): "A resting Pulmonary Capillary Wedge Pressure (PCWP) ≥ 15 mm Hg, or an exercise-induced peak PCWP ≥ 25 mm Hg, provides absolute confirmation of HFpEF."

items.title: Why you must exclude amyloidosis, HCM and constrictive pericarditis before settling on HFpEF
items.content:
Several phenocopies mimic ordinary diastolic failure but need entirely different therapy, so they must be ruled out before finalising a primary HFpEF diagnosis.
Transthyretin cardiac amyloidosis (ATTR-CM): screen with technetium pyrophosphate (⁹⁹ᵐTc-PYP) scintigraphy plus serum/urine immunofixation, especially when there is severe LV hypertrophy or paradoxically low ECG voltage (the 'low voltage with thick walls' clue) — and because disease-modifying therapy now exists.
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) and constrictive pericarditis are the other key structural/physiological mimics to exclude.
keyPoints: ATTR-CM: screen with ⁹⁹ᵐTc-PYP scintigraphy + serum/urine immunofixation
Red flag: severe LVH with low ECG voltage → think amyloid, not just HFpEF
Also exclude HCM and constrictive pericarditis — they need distinct management
items.reference: Important Phenotypic Exclusions: "Transthyretin Cardiac Amyloidosis (ATTR-CM): Screen via technetium pyrophosphate (99mTc-PYP) scintigraphy and serum/urine immunofixation if severe LV hypertrophy or low voltage on ECG is present."

items.title: Correction: the H₂FPEF pulmonary-hypertension threshold is PASP > 35 mmHg, not > 40
items.content:
Original claim (pasted source): the H₂FPEF 'P — Pulmonary Hypertension' variable scores 1 point for a Doppler-estimated PASP > 40 mmHg.
Verification: in the published H₂FPEF score (Reddy et al., Circulation 2018;138:861–870), the pulmonary-hypertension variable is defined as a Doppler-estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure > 35 mmHg (1 point). Multiple secondary sources reproducing the original score confirm the > 35 mmHg cut-point.
Why it matters: using > 40 mmHg makes the criterion harder to satisfy and would under-score borderline pulmonary hypertension, potentially lowering a patient out of the rule-in (≥ 6) range. The correct, score-defining threshold is > 35 mmHg.
keyPoints: Source said PASP > 40 mmHg — the validated H₂FPEF cut-point is > 35 mmHg
Using > 40 would under-score borderline pulmonary hypertension
Apply > 35 mmHg when computing the H₂FPEF 'P' point
items.reference: The H2FPEF Score (Clinical/Echo Rule): "P Pulmonary Hypertension: Doppler-estimated PASP > 40 mm Hg 1 [point]" — corrected to > 35 mmHg per Reddy et al., Circulation 2018.

items.title: Clarification: NT-proBNP 125/365 are referral cut-points, not the HFA-PEFF biomarker Major thresholds
items.content:
Original claim (pasted source): use NT-proBNP > 125 pg/mL (sinus rhythm) or > 365 pg/mL (atrial fibrillation) as the diagnostic biomarker thresholds.
Verification: in the HFA-PEFF biomarker domain (Pieske et al., Eur Heart J 2019), the MAJOR criterion (2 points) is NT-proBNP ≥ 220 pg/mL in sinus rhythm or ≥ 660 pg/mL in AF; the MINOR criterion (1 point) is 125–220 pg/mL (SR) or 365–660 pg/mL (AF). The 125/365 figures the source quotes are the lower (minor / referral) bounds — consistent with the ESC general 'rule-in' referral cut-point of 125 pg/mL — not the major scoring thresholds.
Why it matters: scoring the biomarker domain as '2 points' off a value of only 130 pg/mL would over-weight it. Use ≥ 220 / ≥ 660 for the Major (2-point) biomarker criterion.
keyPoints: Source's 125 (SR)/365 (AF) = minor/referral lower bounds, not Major thresholds
HFA-PEFF biomarker MAJOR (2 pts): NT-proBNP ≥ 220 (SR) / ≥ 660 (AF) pg/mL
HFA-PEFF biomarker MINOR (1 pt): 125–220 (SR) / 365–660 (AF) pg/mL
items.reference: Standard Resting Biomarkers & Diagnostics: "Look for NT-proBNP > 125 pg/mL (or BNP > 35 pg/mL) in sinus rhythm... adjusted upward if the patient is in Atrial Fibrillation (NT-proBNP > 365 pg/mL)" — these are minor/referral bounds; HFA-PEFF Major = ≥ 220 / ≥ 660 pg/mL (Pieske et al., Eur Heart J 2019).

items.question: A 68-year-old obese, hypertensive woman has exertional dyspnoea and an LVEF of 58% with no significant valve disease. Which additional element is REQUIRED to make a definitive diagnosis of HFpEF?
options: Objective evidence of elevated LV filling pressures or diastolic dysfunction
A reduced 6-minute walk distance alone
A body-mass index above 30 kg/m² alone
A normal coronary angiogram
items.explanation: HFpEF requires all three of: HF symptoms/signs, LVEF ≥ 50%, AND objective evidence of cardiac dysfunction (elevated filling pressures or structural/functional diastolic abnormality). She already has symptoms and a preserved EF; the missing mandatory pillar is objective cardiac dysfunction. A walk test, BMI, or a normal angiogram do not satisfy that third criterion.
items.reference: Defining the Diagnostic Framework: "3. Objective Evidence of Cardiac Dysfunction: Non-invasive or invasive documentation of elevated left ventricular (LV) filling pressures, or structural/functional alterations consistent with diastolic dysfunction."

items.question: On a resting echo, the average E/e′ ratio is 16. How is this scored in the HFA-PEFF functional domain?
options: Major functional criterion (2 points)
Minor functional criterion (1 point)
Normal — no points
It is a structural, not functional, criterion
items.explanation: An average E/e′ ≥ 15 is a Major functional criterion worth 2 points, strongly indicating elevated LV filling pressures. An E/e′ of 9–14 would be the Minor (1-point) criterion, and < 8 is normal. E/e′ is a functional (Doppler) parameter, not a structural one.
items.reference: Major Criteria (2 Points Each): "Average E/e' ratio ≥ 15 (Calculated by averaging the septal and lateral early diastolic mitral annular velocities)."

items.question: Which single resting echo finding qualifies as a MAJOR HFA-PEFF functional criterion (2 points)?
options: Peak tricuspid regurgitation velocity > 2.8 m/s
Average E/e′ ratio of 11
LV global longitudinal strain of 15%
LAVI of 31 mL/m² in sinus rhythm
items.explanation: A peak TR velocity > 2.8 m/s is a Major functional criterion (≈ PASP > 31–36 mmHg). An E/e′ of 11 (9–14 range) and GLS < 16% are Minor functional criteria; a LAVI of 31 mL/m² (29–34, SR) is a Minor structural criterion.
items.reference: Major Criteria (2 Points Each): "Peak Tricuspid Regurgitation (TR) velocity > 2.8 m/s (Translates to a continuous-wave Doppler-estimated Pulmonary Artery Systolic Pressure of > 31-36 mm Hg assuming a normal right atrial pressure of 5 mm Hg)."

items.question: A patient in permanent atrial fibrillation has a left atrial volume index (LAVI) of 38 mL/m². Why is the diagnostic threshold for LAVI higher in AF than in sinus rhythm?
options: Chronic AF dilates the atrium regardless of ventricular pathology, so the cut-point is raised to > 40 mL/m² to avoid over-diagnosis
AF shrinks the left atrium, requiring a lower cut-point
LAVI cannot be measured in AF
AF lowers filling pressures, so any LAVI is normal
items.explanation: Chronic AF itself dilates the left atrium independent of LV diastolic disease, so the LAVI threshold is shifted upward to > 40 mL/m² in AF (vs > 34 mL/m² in sinus rhythm) to avoid attributing AF-related atrial enlargement to HFpEF. A LAVI of 38 mL/m² in AF would not reach that raised threshold.
items.reference: Minor Criteria (1 Point Each): "LAVI > 40 mL/m² if the patient is in Atrial Fibrillation (Threshold shifted upward because chronic AF inherently dilates the atrium regardless of ventricular pathology)."

items.question: In the H₂FPEF score, which single variable carries the greatest weight?
options: Atrial fibrillation (3 points)
Body-mass index > 30 kg/m² (2 points)
Age > 60 years (1 point)
Use of ≥ 2 antihypertensive medications (1 point)
items.explanation: Atrial fibrillation is assigned 3 points — the highest of any single H₂FPEF variable and the strongest predictor of HFpEF. Heavy (BMI > 30) is 2 points; hypertension, age and filling-pressure variables are 1 point each.
items.reference: The H2FPEF Score (Clinical/Echo Rule): "F Atrial Fibrillation: Paroxysmal or Persistent 3 [points]"

items.question: A patient's HFA-PEFF score is 3 (intermediate) and the resting echo shows only minor criteria. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Diastolic stress echocardiography (or invasive haemodynamic exercise testing)
Diagnose HFpEF and start therapy immediately
Exclude HFpEF and stop the work-up
Repeat the identical resting echo in 12 months
items.explanation: An intermediate HFA-PEFF score (2–4) with only minor resting criteria is an explicit trigger for dynamic testing, because diastolic abnormalities often manifest only under stress. Diastolic stress echo (a stress E/e′ ≥ 15 or TR velocity > 3.4 m/s confirms HFpEF), or invasive exercise haemodynamics, is the correct next step — not premature diagnosis, premature exclusion, or simply repeating the same resting study.
items.reference: Clinical Corner: "If a standard resting 2D echo shows only minor criteria (leaving the diagnostic score ambiguous), this serves as an explicit trigger to order a Diastolic Stress Echocardiography (Exercise Echo)."

items.question: A 62-year-old man with BMI 41 has exertional dyspnoea, LVEF 55%, and an NT-proBNP of 90 pg/mL. How should the natriuretic peptide be interpreted?
options: A normal NT-proBNP does not exclude HFpEF because obesity falsely suppresses natriuretic peptides
HFpEF is confidently excluded by the normal NT-proBNP
The value mandates an immediate diagnosis of HFpEF
Obesity raises NT-proBNP, so this value is reassuring
items.explanation: Obese patients have falsely suppressed natriuretic peptides due to increased clearance, so a 'normal' NT-proBNP does not exclude HFpEF in a high-BMI patient — exactly this phenotype. The value neither excludes nor confirms HFpEF; further structured assessment (echo/scoring ± stress testing) is needed. Obesity lowers, not raises, NP levels.
items.reference: Standard Resting Biomarkers & Diagnostics: "obese patients frequently exhibit falsely suppressed natriuretic peptide levels due to increased clearance, meaning a 'normal' NT-proBNP does not exclude HFpEF in the presence of a high body mass index."

items.question: During diastolic stress (exercise) echocardiography, which finding fulfils the criteria for a definitive diagnosis of HFpEF?
options: A stress-induced average E/e′ ≥ 15 or a peak TR velocity > 3.4 m/s
Any increase in heart rate above 100 bpm
A fall in E/e′ with exercise
A resting LAVI of 30 mL/m²
items.explanation: On diastolic stress echo, a stress-induced rise in average E/e′ to ≥ 15, or a peak TR velocity > 3.4 m/s, fulfils the criteria for definitive HFpEF. A simple tachycardic response, a fall in E/e′, or a borderline resting LAVI do not.
items.reference: Clinical Corner: "A stress-induced rise in average E/e' ≥ 15 or a peak TR velocity > 3.4 m/s fulfills the criteria for definitive diagnosis."

items.question: On invasive haemodynamic exercise testing for suspected HFpEF, which value provides absolute confirmation?
options: Resting PCWP ≥ 15 mmHg, or exercise peak PCWP ≥ 25 mmHg
Resting PCWP of 8 mmHg
A cardiac index above 2.5 L/min/m²
A right atrial pressure of 4 mmHg
items.explanation: Invasive exercise haemodynamics is the gold standard: a resting pulmonary capillary wedge pressure ≥ 15 mmHg, or an exercise-induced peak PCWP ≥ 25 mmHg, confirms HFpEF. A normal resting PCWP (≈8), a preserved cardiac index, or a normal RA pressure do not confirm it.
items.reference: Invasive Hemodynamic Exercise Testing (The Gold Standard): "A resting Pulmonary Capillary Wedge Pressure (PCWP) ≥ 15 mm Hg, or an exercise-induced peak PCWP ≥ 25 mm Hg, provides absolute confirmation of HFpEF."

items.question: An elderly man labelled 'HFpEF' has severe LV hypertrophy on echo but paradoxically low QRS voltage on ECG. Which work-up is most appropriate before finalising the diagnosis?
options: ⁹⁹ᵐTc-pyrophosphate scintigraphy with serum/urine immunofixation to screen for ATTR cardiac amyloidosis
Immediate initiation of high-dose loop diuretic only
Coronary angiography to exclude ischaemia
Sleep study for obstructive sleep apnoea
items.explanation: Severe LV hypertrophy with low ECG voltage is a classic red flag for transthyretin cardiac amyloidosis (ATTR-CM), a phenocopy requiring distinct therapy. Screening is ⁹⁹ᵐTc-PYP scintigraphy plus serum/urine immunofixation (to exclude AL amyloid). The other tests do not address this specific mimic.
items.reference: Important Phenotypic Exclusions: "Transthyretin Cardiac Amyloidosis (ATTR-CM): Screen via technetium pyrophosphate (99mTc-PYP) scintigraphy and serum/urine immunofixation if severe LV hypertrophy or low voltage on ECG is present."

items.statement: An average E/e′ ratio between 9 and 14 is a MAJOR HFA-PEFF functional criterion.
items.explanation: False. An average E/e′ of 9–14 is an intermediate value scored as a MINOR functional criterion (1 point). The MAJOR functional criterion (2 points) is an average E/e′ ≥ 15.
items.reference: Minor Criteria (1 Point Each): "Average E/e' ratio: 9 to 14"

items.statement: In a markedly obese patient, a normal NT-proBNP reliably excludes HFpEF.
items.explanation: False. Obesity falsely suppresses natriuretic peptides through increased clearance, so a normal NT-proBNP does not exclude HFpEF in a high-BMI patient — a key pitfall, since obesity is a major HFpEF phenotype.
items.reference: Standard Resting Biomarkers & Diagnostics: "a 'normal' NT-proBNP does not exclude HFpEF in the presence of a high body mass index."

items.statement: A resting pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP) ≥ 15 mmHg on invasive testing confirms elevated LV filling pressures consistent with HFpEF.
items.explanation: True. Invasive haemodynamics is the gold standard; a resting PCWP ≥ 15 mmHg (or an exercise-induced peak ≥ 25 mmHg) provides absolute confirmation of the elevated filling pressures that define HFpEF.
items.reference: Invasive Hemodynamic Exercise Testing (The Gold Standard): "A resting Pulmonary Capillary Wedge Pressure (PCWP) ≥ 15 mm Hg, or an exercise-induced peak PCWP ≥ 25 mm Hg, provides absolute confirmation of HFpEF."

items.assertion: HFpEF can be confirmed in a patient whose resting echocardiogram shows only minor criteria.
items.reason: Diastolic abnormalities often manifest only under physiological stress, so a stress-induced average E/e′ ≥ 15 or TR velocity > 3.4 m/s can establish the diagnosis.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because diastolic dysfunction may be unmasked only on exertion, an intermediate/minor resting study is an explicit trigger for diastolic stress echo, where a stress E/e′ ≥ 15 or TR velocity > 3.4 m/s confirms HFpEF. Hence option 0.
items.reference: Clinical Corner: "A stress-induced rise in average E/e' ≥ 15 or a peak TR velocity > 3.4 m/s fulfills the criteria for definitive diagnosis."

items.assertion: In the H₂FPEF score, atrial fibrillation is the most influential single clinical variable.
items.reason: Atrial fibrillation is assigned 3 points, more than any other single variable in the score.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. AF carries 3 points — the highest weight of any single H₂FPEF variable (vs 2 for BMI > 30 and 1 each for hypertension, pulmonary hypertension, age and E/e′ > 9) — and is described as a powerful diagnostic accelerator. Hence option 0.
items.reference: The H2FPEF Score (Clinical/Echo Rule): "F Atrial Fibrillation: Paroxysmal or Persistent 3 [points]"
